name: KB B-Stance Flow (@Asgooch)
description: Unilateral kettlebell flow: B-stance swing → swing clean → B-stance squat → split stance snatch → OH lunge. 5 reps each side, 5 rounds. Don't put the bell down. Video: https://x.com/Asgooch/status/2026011472489324584

sets.name: B-Stance Flow

exercises.notes: B-stance: one foot back, heel up. Swing between legs, explosive hip drive. Bell swings up to about chest height.
→ On the next swing, CATCH it directly into rack position at your shoulder. Don't dead stop — ride the momentum into the clean.
→ Bell is in rack. Stay in B-stance (back foot heel still up). Squat down and stand back up. Bell stays at shoulder.
→ From rack, punch the bell straight overhead in one motion. Lock out arm, stand tall. Split stance snatch — you're still in B-stance when it goes up.
→ Bell locked out overhead, step forward into a lunge. Stand straight up from the lunge with arm STILL locked overhead. That's 1 rep of the full flow.

sets.notes: 5 reps each side × 5 rounds. Don't put the bell down between moves — one continuous chain. Each rep flows: swing → catch into rack → squat → punch overhead → lunge → stand up with arm locked.

tips: Watch the video first: https://x.com/Asgooch/status/2026011472489324584
All same arm, don't put the bell down until you switch sides.
The swing clean is catching a full swing into rack — control the momentum.
Split stance snatch: punch the bell overhead from rack while still in B-stance.
OH lunge: stand straight up with arm STILL locked overhead. That's the hard part.
@Asgooch used a 24kg (53lb) bell. Go lighter to learn the transitions.
Rest 2 min between rounds.

name: Figure-8 → Tactical Clean Complex (@kettleclubfam / kettleclubfam program)
description:
A single-bell hand-switching complex that pairs the figure-8 pass with a tactical clean. The figure-8 passes the bell between the legs and exits into a clean to rack — the pass IS the clean entry. Then a regular tactical clean back through hike and up to rack on the other side. Hand switches happen at the figure-8 pass-through. Source format: 4 sets of descending time / ascending weight pyramid (2:00 @ 12kg → 1:30 @ 16kg → 1:00 @ 20kg → 0:30 @ 24kg), rest 1:00 between sets.

**Pattern per side:**
- START: Hike position (bell between legs, hinged)
- Figure-8 → Tactical Clean (figure-8 between legs, exit-pass becomes the clean to rack)
- Tactical Clean (lower controlled, hike, re-clean to rack)
- END: Hike position
- Switch sides

The pass-through is where you switch hands; the clean is where you build skill at hand catches under load. Bell-baddie vibes.

dayOverview.theme: A figure-8 + tactical clean complex with built-in hand switches. The pass IS the clean entry — the figure-8 exits into a rack catch. Ascending-weight pyramid lets you find your skill ceiling within one session.
watchpoints: **The figure-8 exits INTO the clean — the bell doesn't return to a hand mid-pass.** Pass between legs, hand catches behind, pull becomes the clean to rack. Practice slowly before adding load.
**Tactical clean = controlled, no momentum hack.** The 'tactical' part is deliberate threading/rotation of the wrist into rack. No flopping, no banging the forearm.
**Hand catches under load are the skill.** That's why the source author programs ascending weight — heavier bell forces cleaner catch mechanics.
**Hike position is the home base.** Every transition resets through hike. If you lose the bell, get back to hike before re-starting.
**Ascending weight = descending time** on purpose. Heavier bells get less work time; lighter bells get longer flow time. Don't try to keep 12kg pace at 24kg.

exercises.notes: Light bell, warm the shoulders.
Light bell, get the pass-through pattern grooved before adding the clean.
Light bell, low wide stance. Just the pass — no clean exit yet. Get the hand-switch comfortable.
Hip hinge warm-up. Light bell.

**2 minutes of the complex** at 26lb (12kg) — or whatever your **MEDIUM bell** is per the source author's relative scale.

**Pattern (one compound move, repeated alternating sides):**
- START hike position (bell between legs, hinged)
- **Figure-8 pass exits INTO Tactical Clean to rack** on one side
- Lower bell back through hike
- Next figure-8 pass switches sides → clean to rack on the other side
- Continue alternating

**Pace yourself** — 2:00 is long. Find a sustainable rhythm. The first set is the technique set.

**90 seconds at 35lb (16kg)** — or your **MEDIUM-HEAVY bell**.
Same compound move. Heavier bell = catch mechanics matter more. Slow down before form breaks.

**60 seconds at 44lb (20kg)** — or your **HEAVY bell**.
This is where the catch under load gets real. Bell-baddie territory.

**30 seconds at 53lb (24kg)** — or your **HEAVIEST bell**.

**Note:** Arrilaga max is 50lb — 24kg is right at the ceiling. If 24kg isn't available, hold 20kg (44lb) and add 15-30s of work time to feel the load. The point of set 4 is the heaviest bell you have access to + the cleanest catches you can make at that weight.

Finish strong. Hand catches stay tight.
Light bell, decompress the shoulders.
60s each side. Hike position fries the hip flexors and glutes — pigeon resets them.
60s. Wide-stance work tightens the adductors.

tips: **The figure-8 exits INTO the clean** — that's the whole skill. Pass between legs, hand catches behind, the catch becomes the clean to rack. No 'land and re-grip.'
**Tactical clean = controlled, no momentum hack.** Threading motion into rack, not a yank.
**Ascending weight + descending time** is the source author's intentional pattern. Don't keep 12kg pace at 24kg.
**Hand catches under load** are where the skill builds. That's why the heavy sets are short.
**Hike position is home base.** Every transition resets through hike — if you lose the bell, go back to hike before re-starting.
**Light warmup first** — get the pass-through and clean grooved separately before chaining them.
**Source bell weights** (12/16/20/24kg = 26/35/44/53lb) — pick what you have access to. Arrilaga max is 50lb, so 24kg may not be available — hold 20kg longer if so.
**Cooldown matters** — hike position + figure-8 wide stance = adductor + hip flexor tightness. Pigeon + butterfly resets.
Source: @kettleclubfam (kettleclubfam program).
formPhilosophy: Skill-first KB flow with ascending load. The complex isolates the figure-8-to-clean transition — the moment when a passing pattern becomes a strength catch. Pyramid lets you find the skill ceiling within a single session: long-and-light to lock the pattern, short-and-heavy to test it under load.

name: KB Overhead Mobility Complex (@Asgooch)

Light, test the overhead position
Open hips
Left hand. Controlled pull, full extension.
Left hand. Explosive — bell goes straight overhead.

Left hand. Dip and drive — legs do the work.
Right hand. Same flow.
Right hand. Punch through at the top.
Right hand overhead. Stay stable.
Right hand overhead. Core braced, no arching.
Right hand. Finish strong.

gentle movement

gentle stretch

gentle mobility
hip activation

core stability exercises

name: Prenatal Strength
description: Jess: 30-min bodyweight strength. 3 rounds glutes/legs, 3 rounds upper body, 2 rounds core. Should feel like real work — glutes burn by round 3. NO flat-on-back — toe taps and leg slides done propped on elbows or side-lying.

exercises.notes: Warm up the spine, add hip circles
Wide stance, controlled tempo. Wake up the legs.
Easy warmup bridges — squeeze at top, 2 second hold.
Squeeze hard at top, hold 2 seconds. Last 5 reps: hold at top for 3 seconds.
Harder than it looks — keep hips level. Drop to regular bridge if too much.
Wide stance, slow — 3 seconds down, pause at bottom, 2 seconds up. These should burn by round 3.
Side-lying. Squeeze at the top. Add a hold on the last 5 reps.
Slow and controlled — no swinging. Pulse 5x at the top on last rep.
Slow tempo — 2 seconds down, push up strong. Keep core tight.
Wider hand placement than normal — targets chest more. Controlled tempo.
On knees if needed. Focus on neutral spine — don't let belly sag.
All-fours position. Draw belly button toward spine on exhale, hold, release on inhale.
SIDE-LYING or propped up on elbows — not flat on back. Slow, controlled core stability.
Propped up on elbows or side-lying — not flat on back. Slide one heel out, return. Deep core engagement.
Each side — earned it after that leg work.
Knees wide, breathe deeply.

tips: Jess: this should feel like real work — glutes and legs should burn by round 3
NO flat-on-back — toe taps and leg slides done propped on elbows or side-lying
Slow tempo makes bodyweight exercises much harder — don't rush the reps
Single-leg bridges are challenging — drop to regular bridges if needed
Knee push-ups are a real exercise, not a cop-out — control the descent
Keep breathing throughout — never brace and hold your breath
Modify or skip anything that doesn't feel right — but push yourself where you can

name: Prenatal Stretch & Mobility
description: Jess: 25-min active stretch and mobility. Dynamic warmup then deep holds for hips, lower back, shoulders, calves. NO flat-on-back — knee-to-chest, spinal twist, hamstring stretch all done side-lying. Stay more upright in pigeon to avoid belly compression.

exercises.notes: Add hip circles between reps — figure-8 motion
Standing — reach overhead and bend to each side. Open the ribcage.
Slow, wide stance — pause 2 seconds at the bottom of each rep.
Pedal feet, bend one knee at a time. Active warmup for hamstrings and calves.
Each side — sit tall, lean forward. Try gentle pulses for 10 reps then hold.
Pulse knees 10x then hold. Lean forward from hips.
Each side — use pillows if hip is far from floor. Stay more upright to avoid belly compression.
Deep squat hold — elbows press knees wide. Rock gently side to side.
Each side — sit tall, lean forward. Important for sciatic relief.
Each side — SIDE-LYING position (not flat on back). Gentle pull, keep breathing.
Each side — do SIDE-LYING with pillow between knees. Not flat on back.
Gentle — press up just enough to feel the stretch. Avoid deep backbends.
Knees wide. Walk hands to the left, hold 15s. Walk to the right, hold 15s. Back to center.
Each side — foot on low surface, gentle lean. Keep back flat.
Each side — use wall for balance
Each side — straight leg then bent leg. Helps with nighttime cramps.
Each side — do SIDE-LYING or use towel/strap from reclined with head propped up. Not flat on back.

tips: Jess: dynamic warmup should get blood flowing — don't skip it
NO flat-on-back — knee-to-chest, spinal twist, hamstring stretch all done side-lying
Stay upright in pigeon and forward folds — don't fold deep enough to compress belly
Hip openers will feel more productive as pregnancy progresses and relaxin kicks in
Malasana shows up in every session for a reason — it's the best labor prep stretch
Piriformis stretch helps with sciatic pain — common in second trimester
Calf stretches can prevent nighttime leg cramps

name: Prenatal Yoga Flow
description: Jess: 30-min active prenatal yoga. Warriors, dynamic flows, deep squats, hip openers. Your legs should feel worked. NO flat-on-back (do happy baby side-lying). Avoid folding too deep in pigeon — belly compression. First session took ~38 min with reading time, should be faster now.

exercises.notes: Seated. 4 count in, 6 count out. Set intention.
Sync with breath. Add circles — move hips in figure-8s between reps.
Pedal feet out, warm up hamstrings and calves.
Each side — step forward from down dog. Sink deep, arms overhead. Feel the quads work.
Each side — open hips from warrior one. Arms parallel to floor, gaze over front hand. Hold strong.
Each side — reach long, engage core. Block on floor if needed.
Each side — challenge yourself: close eyes for 10 seconds if balance is good.
Deep squat — press elbows into knees to open hips. Engage pelvic floor on exhale.
Wide stance, slow tempo — 3 seconds down, 1 second hold at bottom, 2 seconds up.
Each side — prop up on block or pillow if needed. Don't fold forward too deep — belly compression. Breathe and sink deeper with each exhale.
Sit tall, press knees toward floor. Try pulsing 10x then hold.
Do this SIDE-LYING — flat on back causes too much belly pressure. Rock gently side to side.
One more round — this time hands in prayer, elbows pressing knees wide.
Knees wide for belly. Arms forward, forehead down. Let everything go.
Side-lying. Full relaxation.

tips: Jess: this is an active yoga session — your legs should feel worked by the end
Warrior holds build real leg strength — don't rush them
Malasana (deep squat) is the #1 prenatal pose — practice it daily
Use blocks and pillows freely but challenge yourself where you can
NO flat-on-back poses — do happy baby side-lying, spinal twists with pillow between knees
Belly compression: don't fold too deep in pigeon or forward folds — stay upright-ish
If balance is shaky, keep one hand on a wall — no shame in support
Second time through will be faster — first session took ~38 min with reading time

name: Swim Day 1: Drill Day
description: Jess: Technique-focused swim — all drills, smooth strokes, feel the water. ~35 min, 1500-1800y. The kind of practice where you get out feeling like you swim better than when you got in.

exercises.notes: Easy free — long strokes, find your rhythm. Open turns.
Loosen up the shoulders, easy kick.
Focus on full extension before the next stroke. Glide.
25 fist / 25 open. Feel the difference — the open hand 25 should feel like you have paddles on.
Stay long on each side. Hips and shoulders rotate together.
25 right / 25 left. Non-working arm extended in front.
High elbows, relaxed recovery. Let the fingertips barely skim the surface.
Smooth and long. Focus on applying what you drilled — high catch, full rotation. Enjoy the buoyancy.
Easy backstroke, let the heart rate come down.
Lazy sidestroke — you earned it.

tips: NO flip turns — open turns only, no crunching the belly
NO butterfly — skip it entirely during pregnancy
Keep effort conversational — you should be able to talk between sets
Stay hydrated — bring a water bottle to the pool deck
If any drill feels weird or uncomfortable, swap it for easy free
Pool temp should be 78-84°F — avoid hot tubs and very warm pools

name: Swim Day 2: Kick Party
description: Jess: Kick-heavy day — legs, hips, glutes, core. ~30 min, 1200-1500y. Board work, back kick, breast kick. Legs will feel it. Hip openers built in via breaststroke kick.

exercises.notes: Easy free, wake up the body.
Long glides, open up the hips.
Steady flutter kick. Compact, from the hips. No death grip on the board.
Kick — squeeze — gliiiiide. Count to 3 on each glide. Hip opener city.
No board, arms at sides. Relax the neck, look at the ceiling, kick easy.
Surface dolphin kick on your back. Gentle wave motion. Skip if back feels off.
Build effort: 50 easy / 50 moderate / 50 moderate / 50 easy. Like a wave.
Easy backstroke to recover.
Easy free — smooth and long.
Lazy sidestroke, alternate sides at 50.

tips: Breaststroke kick is amazing for pregnancy hip mobility — lean into it
If flutter kick aggravates the lower back, switch to back kick or breast kick
Keep the dolphin kick gentle — surface only, soft undulation
Bring a water bottle — kicking is sneaky cardio
NO flip turns, NO butterfly

name: Swim Day 3: Pull & Chill
description: Jess: Upper body focus with pull buoy — let the legs rest after kick day. ~30 min, 1200-1500y. Smooth pulls, sculling, backstroke. Chill vibes.

exercises.notes: Easy backstroke to start — face out of the water, find your rhythm.
Smooth free, long strokes.
Buoy between thighs. Long, smooth pulls. Focus on the catch — fingertips down, elbow high. Rest :15 between.
Pull buoy backstroke — so floaty. Steady rotation, pinky-first entry.
Scull on your back: head-first, feet-first, mid-scull, dealer's choice. Small figure-8s with the hands.
Catch-up to reinforce that long front-end feeling.
Easy breast — long glides, open hips.
Sidestroke home. Switch sides at 50.

tips: Pull buoy takes pressure off the legs — enjoy the float
The buoy lifts your hips which feels amazing with a growing belly
Sculling is meditation in the pool — let yourself zone out
If you only have 20 min, skip the sculling block and just do the pull set
NO flip turns, NO butterfly

name: Swim Day 4: Mix Tape
description: Jess: Variety day — a little of everything, nothing too long. ~35 min, 1500-1800y. Stroke medleys, drill/swim combos, mixed kick. The 'never bored' workout.

exercises.notes: 50 back / 50 breast / 100 free. Easy, get all the strokes moving.
25 catch-up drill / 25 swim. Feel the difference.
25 fingertip drag / 25 swim. High elbows should carry over.
25 right arm / 25 left arm.
Put it all together — swim with all those cues in your head.
Moderate effort back.
Moderate effort breast — gliiiiide on each one.
Moderate effort free — build to 75% on the last 50.
50 flutter / 50 breast kick. Variety!
Easy back kick, arms at sides. Relax into it.
Easy sidestroke.
Lazy backstroke — float home.

tips: This is the 'never get bored' workout — nothing lasts long enough to get tedious
Drill/swim combos: the drill teaches your body, the swim lets you apply it
If you're short on time, cut the kick snack and go straight to cooldown
NO flip turns, NO butterfly, NO diving

name: Swim Day 5: Long & Easy
description: Jess: Steady-state endurance swim — longer sets, easy effort. ~35 min, 1800-2000y. Zone out, find your flow, rack up the yards. The meditative one.

exercises.notes: Easy 300 free. No rush. Find your breathing pattern and settle in.
Steady, conversational effort. Breathe every 3 or every 4 — whatever's comfortable. :20 rest between. Just swim.
Pull buoy — let the legs rest. Smooth, easy pulls.
Pull buoy backstroke — float city.
Easy backstroke — different muscles, same chill effort.
Breast with looong glides. Count your strokes per length — try to lower the count.
Sidestroke — the laziest cooldown in swimming.

tips: This is the zen swim — long sets, easy effort, zone out
If 150s feel too long, break them into 100s with :10 rest
Counting strokes per length is a fun game to stay engaged during longer sets
Breathe as often as you need — every 2 is totally fine during pregnancy
NO flip turns, NO butterfly

name: Swim Day 6: Fast & Short
description: Jess: Short intervals with lots of rest — the closest to 'speed work' that's safe. ~30 min, 1200-1500y. 25s and 50s with plenty of recovery. Gets the competitive swimmer brain happy.

exercises.notes: Easy free to warm up.
Easy back, loosen the shoulders.
Drill to activate the catch before the main set.
FAST 25s — not all-out, but 80% effort. Like race pace from back in the day. :20 rest between each. This is the fun part.
Moderate-hard 50s — 70% effort. Focus on turnover and clean strokes. :30 rest between.
Moderate back — keep the tempo up but controlled. :20 rest.
Easy breast kick to flush the legs. Long glides.
Back kick, totally relaxed.
Easy sidestroke to bring it home.

tips: 80% effort on the fast 25s — this isn't a race, it's remembering what fast feels like
Plenty of rest between intervals — take more if you need it
Heart rate should come back down during rest before the next one
If you feel breathless or dizzy, dial it back to 60% or skip to the kick flush
The breast kick cooldown after speed work feels incredible
NO flip turns, NO diving off the blocks (tempting, I know), NO butterfly

name: Swim Day 7: Recovery & Play
description: Jess: Active recovery day — treading, sculling, easy strokes, pool walking. ~25 min, 800-1000y. Low key, playful, feel good. Or take the day off — either is perfect.

exercises.notes: Super easy backstroke. No rush. Enjoy being weightless.
Sculling in all directions — head-first, feet-first, mid-scull. Get creative. Remember doing these at practice?
2 min of easy treading — egg-beater or flutter, whatever feels good. Chat with a lane-mate if you have one.
1 min vertical kick — arms crossed. Sneaky good core work. Drop arms into the water if it's too much.
Breaststroke — count your strokes per length. Can you do it in fewer?
Sidestroke — alternate sides. Channel your inner lifeguard.
Easy free — try breathing bilaterally just for fun.
5 min pool walking in the shallow end — forward, backward, sideways. Water resistance = sneaky workout. The buoyancy feels amazing on a pregnant body.

tips: This is the 'just get in the water' day — zero pressure
Pool walking is genuinely great exercise during pregnancy — zero impact, full support
If you're tired, skip this entirely and rest — rest is also training
Vertical kick and treading are old-school water polo drills — great core work
The water supports ~90% of your body weight — enjoy feeling light
NO flip turns, NO butterfly, NO guilt if you skip this day
